description: A 'tapulo' is a rigid, wide-mouthed vessel used to keep food or other small material. It often takes a lid, though closure and preservation method remain separate. A bottle pours through a narrow neck; a jar opens wide enough for a hand or spoon.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip against the backs of the upper teeth for unvoiced dental 't' and release into central 'a'. Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and hold stressed high-back rounded 'u'. Touch the tongue tip to the alveolar ridge for 'l', let air pass along its sides, and finish on true-mid rounded 'o'.
sound_symbolism: Dental 'ta' and stressed bilabial 'pu' make 'tapu' (close) audible before final 'lo'. That last syllable releases the tongue into a broader rounded chamber. Two firm contacts open into the hollow they have enclosed.
usage_notes: Jars are countable. Contents, material, lid, and storage place fit ordinary phrases. Food safety depends on the particular condition and method rather than on the vessel's name.

pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: One household jar kept salt near the hearth and grain from mice. Another carried preserved food through the cold months.

semantic_domains.creation: a rigid storage vessel with a broad mouth
semantic_domains.physical: a holder reached through an opening wider than a bottle neck
